career.notes: 102 patents from some three dozen inventions

narrative_blocks.header: A daughter’s death built better helmets
narrative_blocks.body: After his daughter Wendy died in a skiing accident at Mammoth Mountain, he and her sisters founded Team Wendy — ski helmets first, then the energy-absorbing pads inside Marine and Army helmets. Wendy Park on Whiskey Island, one of Ohio’s busiest, carries her name.

narrative_blocks.header: One hundred two patents and counting
narrative_blocks.body: His first was a drain plug for a flooded Ford floor, dictated as a tax write-off on a ski trip — Ford grabbed it. Thirteen companies later, as a Metroparks commissioner he pressed to open the secluded lakefront: “Where the land meets the shore is sacred.”

career.role: Securities Commissioner of Ohio; author of the Ohio Securities Act

career.organization: SEC Cleveland (Zone 4: OH, MI, IN, KY)

career.role: Civilian major, regional counterintelligence chief, Cairo

career.notes: fees to $20,000 a speech; syndicated fraud column

narrative_blocks.body: The President offered the White House for his birth; his mother declined, and Roosevelt — his father’s commander and his cousin Edith’s husband — stood godfather instead.

A civilian major in Donovan’s OSS, he sat overtly as deputy to the Middle East economic mission while covertly running the region’s counterintelligence — choking Germany’s industrial-diamond supply, foiling an assassination plot against King George II of Greece, and inventing a hoist for airlifting spies.

The Terrible Game (1957), grown from a Saturday Evening Post story, became the 1985 cult film Gymkata; the Navy put his Cloak and Cipher on every warship. He launched a trade firm with Eliot Ness and grew the International Platform Association past ten thousand members.

narrative_blocks.header: His treaty gave the OSS half the world
narrative_blocks.body: Moore hammered out the treaty between Donovan and his arch-rival J. Edgar Hoover that handed the OSS the eastern hemisphere and the FBI the western. Thirty years on, CIA Director William Colby accepted his lecture invitation, “interested in clarifying the real nature of modern American intelligence.”

cross_connections.display_label: ran Army G-2 while he spied for its rival, the OSS

bio.bio_blurb: Sculptress heiress of the Franklin Steamship line

narrative_blocks.header: She came to the party with someone else
narrative_blocks.body: Betty Oakes arrived at the Kirtland Club party with a lawyer; Dan Moore had come to Cleveland to visit his Yale roommate Howard Hanna of the mining family. A Smith-educated sculptress and daughter of the Franklin Steamship founder, she married Moore in 1932, the year he left Yale.

bio.bio_blurb: Founder of the Army’s artillery school

career.organization: US Army School of Fire, Fort Sill

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Army’s artillery school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1911 Moore became founder and first commandant of the US Army School of Fire at Fort Sill, Oklahoma — today’s Field Artillery School. He had served as White House military aide to Theodore Roosevelt, husband of his first cousin Edith.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Lucerne between White House seasons
narrative_blocks.body: Washington society followed the aide’s November 1906 wedding to Luvie Butler in Lucerne, Switzerland — two months’ leave, returning “just at the opening of the social season.” She died in 1915, leaving him three children under ten.

bio.bio_blurb: White House wedding-season bride; died at twenty-nine

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-nine, three children small
narrative_blocks.body: “On Thursday, March 11, 1915 at 3 A.M.,” ran the Washington Post notice, “Luvie Jones Butler, beloved wife of Captain Dan T. Moore” — private interment at Arlington. Dan Jr. was seven, Luvie five, Francesca two; their father raised them a decade before remarrying.
